description: V2 oracle test facts for Q_f invariants and 3D Navier-Stokes regularity. Length-balanced distractors (ratio 0.8-1.2x) to eliminate length bias.

facts.context: The Beale-Kato-Majda (1984) criterion states that a smooth 3D Euler/Navier-Stokes solution blows up at time T if and only if:
facts.truth: ∫₀ᵀ ||ω||_{L^∞} dt = ∞ (vorticity maximum becomes infinite in finite time)
distractors: ∫₀ᵀ ||∇p||_{L²} dt = ∞ (pressure gradient norm diverges in finite time)
∫₀ᵀ ||u||_{L^∞} dt = ∞ (velocity field maximum becomes unbounded at T)
∫₀ᵀ ||∇u||_{L²} dt = ∞ (velocity gradient integrated norm blows up)

facts.context: The 3D vorticity equation ∂ω/∂t + (u·∇)ω = (ω·∇)u + ν∇²ω contains the term (ω·∇)u which is called:
facts.truth: vortex stretching (unique to 3D, absent in 2D, can amplify |ω|)
distractors: vortex advection (present in both 2D and 3D, transports |ω|)
viscous diffusion (smooths vorticity gradients, dampens |ω|)
baroclinic torque (generates vorticity from pressure gradients)

facts.context: For 3D Euler vorticity, under vortex stretching by factor s (tube stretches, thins), the standard Q_f = ∫∫ ω(x)·ω(y) f(|x-y|) d³x d³y transforms as:
facts.truth: quadratic growth in s (Q_f scales as s² under axial stretching)
distractors: linear growth in s (Q_f scales proportionally to the stretch factor)
logarithmic growth in s (Q_f scales as ln(s) under axial stretching)
inverse decay in s (Q_f scales as 1/s under the stretching deformation)

facts.context: In 3D Euler, the kinetic energy E = (1/2)∫|u|²dx corresponds to Q_{1/r}. Under vortex stretching, energy is:
facts.truth: exactly conserved (stretching redistributes but cannot create energy)
distractors: quadratically amplified (stretching generates energy proportionally)
slowly dissipated (stretching gradually converts energy to entropy)
approximately conserved (small losses accumulate during stretching)

facts.context: The ratio R_f = Q_{e^(-r)} / Q_{1/r} for 3D vortex dynamics under stretching is:
facts.truth: more stable than either Q_f alone (both scale as s², ratio partially cancels)
distractors: more sensitive than either Q_f alone (ratio amplifies s² scaling further)
completely independent of the stretching factor s (ratio is exactly one)
undefined when either Q_f diverges under large stretching deformations

facts.context: Numerical tests of R_f = Q_{exp} / Q_{inv} on 3D vortex ring evolution show stretch resistance of:
facts.truth: 2% variation under stretching (vs 60% for standard Q_f)
distractors: 60% variation under stretching (comparable to standard Q_f)
0.001% variation under stretching (machine precision exact)
35% variation under stretching (moderate improvement over Q_f)

facts.context: For 3D Euler, the enstrophy-normalized Q_f / Ω (where Ω = ∫|ω|² dx) behaves under stretching as:
facts.truth: 17% variation (better than 60% for unnormalized, worse than R_f at 2%)
distractors: 0.1% variation (nearly exact conservation, outperforming all R_f ratios)
60% variation (enstrophy normalization provides no improvement at all)
45% variation (marginal improvement over unnormalized standard form)

facts.context: The curvature-weighted Q_f^κ = ∫∫ κ(x)κ(y) ω·ω f(r) d³x d³y shows stretch resistance of:
facts.truth: 4% variation (emphasizes bent regions, reduces when tubes straighten)
distractors: 50% variation (curvature weighting destabilizes the invariant further)
0.01% variation (curvature weighting produces near-exact conservation)
25% variation (comparable performance to enstrophy normalization)

facts.context: For 2D Navier-Stokes with viscosity ν, the Q_f quantities decay as:
facts.truth: dQ_f/dt ∝ -ν (linear in viscosity), recovering Euler conservation as ν→0
distractors: dQ_f/dt ∝ -ν² (quadratic in viscosity), vanishing faster than linear decay
dQ_f/dt ∝ -ν^(3/2) (fractional power), intermediate between linear/quadratic
dQ_f/dt ∝ -√ν (square root of viscosity), decaying slower than linear rate

facts.context: For a Gaussian vortex with width σ and circulation Γ, the relationship between Q_r and ||ω||_∞ is:
facts.truth: ||ω||_∞ ∝ 1/Q_r² (if Q_r conserved, vorticity peak bounded)
distractors: ||ω||_∞ ∝ Q_r² (vorticity peak grows quadratically with Q_r)
||ω||_∞ ∝ exp(Q_r) (vorticity peak grows exponentially with Q_r)
||ω||_∞ ∝ Q_r^(1/2) (vorticity peak grows as square root of Q_r)

facts.context: For 2D Euler/Navier-Stokes, Q_f conservation combined with BKM implies:
facts.truth: no finite-time blowup (consistent with known 2D global regularity)
distractors: finite-time blowup is possible (Q_f cannot prevent 2D singularities)
only local-in-time existence (Q_f bounds are too weak to extend globally)
conditional regularity only (Q_f requires additional smallness assumptions)

facts.context: The main obstacle to using Q_f for 3D NS regularity is:
facts.truth: vortex stretching breaks Q_f conservation (except for energy Q_{1/r})
distractors: viscous dissipation breaks Q_f conservation (except for enstrophy Q_r²)
pressure terms break Q_f conservation (except for helicity Q_{curl})
boundary conditions break Q_f conservation (except for momentum Q_lin)

facts.context: Physically, the ratio R_f = Q_{e^(-r)} / Q_{1/r} measures:
facts.truth: locality of vorticity interactions (how much dynamics depends on nearby vs distant vorticity)
distractors: rate of enstrophy dissipation (how quickly vorticity variance decays over time)
helicity density distribution (how vortex tube linking varies across scales)
energy cascade direction (whether energy transfers to larger or smaller scales)

facts.context: In 3D ideal fluids, the helicity H = ∫ u·ω dx is:
facts.truth: exactly conserved (topological invariant of vortex line linking)
distractors: approximately conserved (slowly decays due to nonlinear interactions)
not conserved in general (only preserved for axisymmetric geometries)
monotonically increasing (grows under vortex stretching amplification)

facts.context: The Clay Millennium Problem for 3D Navier-Stokes asks whether:
facts.truth: smooth solutions remain smooth for all time, or if singularities can develop
distractors: weak solutions are unique given arbitrary square-integrable initial velocity data
turbulent flows converge to statistical equilibrium in the infinite time limit
viscous solutions approach inviscid Euler solutions as viscosity tends to zero

facts.context: The combined score (stretch + evolution conservation) for R_f = Q_{exp}/Q_{inv} is:
facts.truth: 0.59% (best among all tested variants including enstrophy-norm and curvature-weighted)
distractors: 2.44% (second-best after curvature-weighted Q_f which achieves 0.31% combined)
6.40% (third-best, outperformed by both enstrophy-norm and standard variants)
12.7% (worst among tested variants, standard Q_f outperforms at 2.95% combined)

facts.context: Escauriaza-Seregin-Sverak (2003) proved that 3D Navier-Stokes singularity formation requires:
facts.truth: L3 velocity norm blows up (critical scaling-invariant Lebesgue space)
distractors: L2 velocity norm blows up (energy-level Lebesgue space diverges)
pressure develops a point singularity (local pressure blowup)
H1 vorticity norm blows up (first Sobolev norm of vorticity)

facts.context: The pressure Hessian ∂²p/∂xᵢ∂xⱼ in 3D Navier-Stokes vorticity dynamics is:
facts.truth: non-local (determined by Poisson equation coupling all vorticity to strain globally)
distractors: purely local (depends only on vorticity and strain at each point)
only relevant in 2D flows (vanishes identically in 3D geometry)
negligible at high Reynolds number (viscous term dominates instead)

facts.context: The scaling-critical Sobolev space for 3D Navier-Stokes (invariant under the natural scaling λu(λ²t, λx)) is:
facts.truth: H^{1/2} (half-derivative Sobolev space, same scaling as L3)
distractors: H1 (one full derivative, the energy dissipation space)
L2 (energy space, where Leray-Hopf weak solutions live)
H^{-1} (negative Sobolev space, dual of H1 velocity space)

facts.context: The enstrophy evolution equation for 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes d/dt(½∫|ω|²dx) contains:
facts.truth: exactly two terms: vortex stretching ∫ωᵢSᵢⱼωⱼdx and viscous dissipation -ν∫|∇ω|²dx, with no explicit pressure term
distractors: three terms: vortex stretching, viscous dissipation, and an explicit pressure gradient source term
exactly two terms: an explicit pressure Hessian source and viscous dissipation with no stretching
four terms: stretching, dissipation, pressure work, and a baroclinic production contribution

facts.context: In the 3D enstrophy equation, the role of pressure is:
facts.truth: indirect: pressure enters through the strain tensor S via the incompressibility constraint ∇·u=0
distractors: direct: pressure appears as an explicit source term ∫ω·∇²p dx in the enstrophy equation
absent: pressure has no effect whatsoever on enstrophy dynamics in incompressible flow
dominant: the pressure Hessian term exceeds vortex stretching at all Reynolds numbers

facts.context: Axisymmetric 3D Navier-Stokes without swirl (u_θ = 0) has:
facts.truth: global regularity for all smooth initial data (proven by Ladyzhenskaya 1968, Ukhovskii-Yudovich 1968)
distractors: global regularity only for sufficiently small initial data (large data may still blow up)
global regularity only in the energy class (stronger norms may still become singular)
no regularity result beyond local existence (open problem like the general 3D case)

facts.context: For axisymmetric Navier-Stokes WITHOUT swirl, the quantity ω_θ/r satisfies:
facts.truth: a maximum principle: |ω_θ/r| cannot exceed its initial maximum, which controls regularity
distractors: no maximum principle: ω_θ/r can grow without bound even from smooth initial conditions
a maximum principle only for small initial data (large ω_θ/r can still blow up)
a maximum principle only in 2D, not in the axisymmetric 3D reduction geometry

facts.context: Axisymmetric 3D Navier-Stokes WITH swirl (u_θ ≠ 0) differs from without swirl because:
facts.truth: the maximum principle for ω_θ/r is destroyed by the swirl term, making regularity an open problem
distractors: energy conservation fails with swirl, removing the fundamental a priori bound on solutions
the swirl introduces a new conserved quantity that conflicts with regularity estimates
the vorticity equation gains extra dimensions, making it equivalent to the full 3D problem

facts.context: Under the natural NS scaling u→λu(λx,λ²t), the enstrophy ∫|∇u|²dx = ∫|ω|²dx scales as:
facts.truth: λ^1 (supercritical), meaning enstrophy bounds alone cannot control regularity in 3D
distractors: λ^0 (scaling-invariant), making enstrophy the natural critical quantity for regularity
λ^{-1} (subcritical), meaning enstrophy bounds are stronger than needed for regularity
λ^2 (strongly supercritical), but this is compensated by viscous dissipation scaling

facts.context: The critical dissipation exponent for fractional 3D Navier-Stokes with (-Δ)^α dissipation (Lions 1969) is:
facts.truth: α = 5/4: global regularity is proven for α ≥ 5/4, and the regularity question is open for α < 5/4
distractors: α = 1 (the standard Laplacian), below which regularity fails immediately
α = 3/2, matching the scaling of the H^{3/2} Sobolev space
α = 1/2, matching the scaling-critical H^{1/2} space

facts.context: The Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg theorem (1982) constrains the singular set of suitable Leray-Hopf weak solutions of 3D NS:
facts.truth: the one-dimensional parabolic Hausdorff measure of the singular set is zero, so singularities cannot form curves in spacetime
distractors: the three-dimensional Hausdorff measure of the singular set is zero, which allows line singularities
the singular set is empty, proving global regularity for weak solutions
the singular set has Hausdorff dimension at most 2, allowing surface singularities

facts.context: The Prodi-Serrin regularity condition 2/p + 3/q = 1 for 3D Navier-Stokes is:
facts.truth: exactly the scaling-critical curve: every point satisfying 2/p + 3/q = 1 gives an L^p_t L^q_x norm invariant under NS scaling
distractors: a supercritical condition that provides more control than needed for regularity
a subcritical condition that explains why it can prevent blowup
unrelated to scaling because it involves mixed space-time norms

facts.context: The Green's function of the fractional Laplacian (-Δ)^{5/4} in R^3 is proportional to:
facts.truth: r^{-1/2} (since G ∝ r^{2α-3} = r^{2(5/4)-3} = r^{-1/2} for the critical exponent α=5/4)
distractors: r^{-1} (same as the standard Laplacian Green's function in 3D)
-ln(r) (the 2D Laplacian Green's function, not applicable in 3D)
r^{-3/2} (corresponding to the fractional Laplacian (-Δ)^{9/4} in 3D)

facts.context: The scaling-critical Q_f kernel for 3D NS (where Q_f = ∫∫ω·ω f(|x-y|) d³xd³y) is:
facts.truth: f(r) = r^{-2}, because vorticity scaling (λ²) shifts the critical kernel from r^{-1/2} (velocity-based) to r^{-2} (vorticity-based)
distractors: f(r) = r^{-1/2}, matching the Green's function of the critical fractional Laplacian (-Δ)^{5/4}
f(r) = r^{-1}, matching the standard 3D Laplacian Green's function (energy kernel)
f(r) = -ln(r), matching the 2D Green's function transplanted to 3D

facts.context: Under 3D NS scaling u→λu(λx,λ²t), the helicity H = ∫u·ω dx scales as:
facts.truth: λ^0 (scaling-critical), because u→λ¹ and ω→λ² and d³x→λ⁻³ giving λ^{1+2-3}=λ^0
distractors: λ^{-1} (subcritical, same scaling as kinetic energy)
λ^{+1} (supercritical, same scaling as enstrophy)
λ^{-2} (strongly subcritical, irrelevant for regularity)

facts.context: Despite being scaling-critical and conserved in 3D Euler, helicity H cannot control regularity for general flows because:
facts.truth: H is a signed (indefinite) quantity that allows cancellation between regions of opposite helicity, so |H| does not bound the H^{1/2} norm
distractors: helicity is not actually conserved in viscous Navier-Stokes flow
helicity has the wrong scaling to control any critical Sobolev norm
helicity conservation is too weak because it only constrains topology, not amplitude

facts.context: Biferale and Titi (2013) proved global regularity for a helical-decimated 3D NS model by showing that:
facts.truth: restricting to sign-definite helicity gives a conserved quantity equivalent to the H^{1/2} norm, and conservation of this critical norm implies regularity
distractors: the nonlinear term vanishes entirely for single-helical-sector flows
the decimated model reduces to 2D dynamics regardless of spatial dimension
an additional viscosity term appears that provides extra dissipation

facts.context: The Biferale-Titi result implies that the 3D NS regularity problem reduces to understanding:
facts.truth: cross-helicity interactions (u+ coupling with u-), which are the only mechanism that can break the H^{1/2} conservation that guarantees regularity in single-sector flows
distractors: the energy cascade rate, which determines whether energy reaches small scales fast enough to cause blowup
the viscous dissipation mechanism, which may fail to prevent singularity at extreme Reynolds numbers
the pressure term, which is the only nonlocal element in the Navier-Stokes equations

facts.context: Under 3D NS scaling u→λu(λx,λ²t), the kinetic energy E = ½∫|u|²dx scales as:
facts.truth: λ^{-1} (subcritical), meaning energy bounds are weaker than what is needed to control regularity
distractors: λ^0 (scaling-invariant), making energy the natural critical quantity
λ^1 (supercritical), same scaling as enstrophy
λ^{-2} (strongly subcritical), explaining why energy conservation is insufficient
